[25:21] us nurses, especially shift workers, police
[25:26] officers, EMS people, like anybody that's eating
[25:30] on the run is very likely to chew poorly because
[25:34] they're eating quickly.
[25:36] Floor nurses are one of the worst eaters on
[25:39] the planet.
[25:40] Number one, they'll skip meals when they're
[25:41] starving and they're stressed and they're working
[25:45] 12-hour shifts back to back.
[25:47] That creates terrible gut dysfunction.

[25:53] down food in five or ten minutes in between

[26:26] Because all of those things are creating
[26:28] stressful situations and all of those are
[26:30] signaling terrible digestion.
[26:35] Make sure when they swallow that they are
[26:37] swallowing small bites, not huge chunks,
[26:40] because when food passes the epiglottis,
[26:43] that's a choke hazard.
[26:45] And so anybody that's telling you they're
[26:46] choking frequently, that's a person you want
[26:49] to say, hey, tell me a little bit more
[26:50] about what it's like when you eat.
[26:53] Talk to them about that methodology of how
[26:56] food is getting in their system.
[26:58] Remember the esophageal sphincter is
[26:59] supposed to sit below the diaphragm,
[27:03] not above.
[27:04] And so when the esophageal sphincter or
[27:06] any other of the stomach tissue comes up,
[27:09] that becomes a hernia.
[27:11] That's what is called or known as a
[27:12] hiatal hernia.
[27:14] This is the diaphragm.
[27:15] This is your esophagus or the sphincter
[27:18] coming up into the esophagus that is
[27:20] supposed to stay down here.
[27:22] Not push through the diaphragm.
[27:24] When it pushes through the diaphragm,
[27:25] we now have a hiatal hernia.
[27:28] So it's important that hiatal hernias
[27:30] be addressed.
[27:31] The most common cause of a hiatal
[27:33] hernia is constipated stool.
[27:37] Yes, you heard me right.
[27:39] The most common cause of reflux,
[27:41] heartburn, and digestion is
[27:43] constipated stool.
[27:45] So we're going to talk about that
[27:47] some more.
[27:48] Look at the stomach.
[27:50] Are they supplying enough chloride
[27:53] from their salt intake to make
[27:55] appropriate hydrochloric acid?
[27:58] People that eat low salt don't have
[28:00] good acid production.
[28:02] Many of them and their GI docs will
[28:06] also tell them they're making
[28:09] too much acid when in fact,
[28:11] that's not the truth at all.
[28:12] They're actually not making enough acid
[28:15] because sodium chloride is what
[28:18] helps contribute to hydrochloric acid
[28:20] production.
[28:21] So remember your chemistry.
[28:23] Sodium is a positive ion.
[28:25] Chloride is negative and when we
[28:29] consume salt as a combined molecule,
[28:32] it gets digested and separated.
[28:34] Sodium goes over here and does
[28:36] work with mussels and potassium
[28:38] and all kinds of other things.
[28:39] Chloride goes off on its way to
[28:42] work to produce hydrochloric acid.
[28:45] And so mixed with the food that
[28:48] hydrochloric acid begins to form
[28:49] the kind ball that is then moved
[28:52] when we swallow and mussels begin
[28:55] to contract, moving that through
[28:58] into the small intestine.
[29:01] So in the small intestines,
[29:02] lots of absorption is happening.
[29:05] In the large intestines,
[29:07] mostly what you're absorbing is
[29:08] water.
[29:09] There's a few ingredients that
[29:11] continue to be absorbed
[29:12] nutritionally, but the majority
[29:14] of the time, large intestines
[29:15] are just absorbing water.
[29:17] So watery stool literally enters
[29:21] the large intestine near your
[29:22] appendix at the bottom right.
[29:25] If you're looking at the screen,
[29:26] it's on the left side because
[29:27] you're facing the body, but
[29:29] it's on your physical right
[29:31] side near your leg.
[29:33] You're growing on the right
[29:34] side where your appendix is,
[29:36] is where watery stool from the
[29:38] small intestine enters the
[29:39] large intestine.
[29:42] Miraculously,
[29:44] this works perfectly when it
[29:47] works well.
[29:49] So watery stool begins to flow
[29:52] upward through the ascending
[29:54] colon while water is being
[29:58] resorbed, recirculated, sent
[30:00] around to other parts of the
[30:02] body to do its job.
[30:05] As that stool is pushed upward,
[30:10] the body begins to form
[30:12] stool. All these little
[30:15] pockets all along the colon
[30:17] begin to absorb.
[30:19] They're sucking up the waste
[30:21] into these little pockets all
[30:23] along the gut, and then
[30:24] eventually those muscular
[30:29] pockets are going to push waste
[30:31] on up and across the transverse
[30:34] colon and then down into the
[30:36] sigmoid colon and out into
[30:37] the toilet.
[30:39] That is how good
[30:41] muscles are supposed to work.
[30:43] If that's not happening,
[30:45] we need to figure out why.
[30:47] Okay,
[30:49] so think about all of those
[30:51] things when you have a patient
[30:53] that's talking to you about
[30:55] gut health.
[30:57] So number one, what should
[30:58] waste look like? Get you a
[31:00] Bristol stool form scale.
[31:01] There's PDF documents all over
[31:03] the internet.
[31:04] You can get cute ones for
[31:06] little kids, plain ones for
[31:09] older people and adults,
[31:12] but have them chart what their
[31:14] poop looks like every day for
[31:16] four weeks or every week or
[31:17] whatever. If they are skipping
[31:19] multiple days a week, they are
[31:20] constipated.
[31:22] They should have logs that are
[31:24] shaped mostly like a snake.
[31:27] If they are having hard balls
[31:30] in the midst of their poop,
[31:32] that is constipated stool.
[31:34] Those are the little hard balls
[31:36] are forming when the water is
[31:38] absorbed as the waste is pulled
[31:42] up into the pockets and it
[31:43] literally gets stuck there.
[31:45] Those balls of poop literally
[31:47] get stuck inside those pockets
[31:49] and they're not moving through
[31:50] appropriately.
[31:51] If they're having watery
[31:52] stool, that is not appropriate
[31:54] either.
[31:55] Nobody should have watery
[31:56] stool.
[31:56] Nobody should have hard ball
[31:57] stool.
[31:58] If they're straining, if
[32:00] they're pushing, if they
[32:03] are skipping days, all of
[32:05] that is not healthy.
[32:07] Typically, four is your perfect
[32:10] stool form on the Bristol scale.
[32:12] It's snake-like.
[32:15] You can have some threes and
[32:17] some fives and still have fairly
[32:20] normal healthy digestion.
[32:22] But if your patient is
[32:23] reporting ones and twos or
[32:25] sixes and sevens on the Bristol
[32:26] scale, they have some gut work
[32:28] to do and so that's going to
[32:30] be something that we have to
[32:32] look into.
[32:34] In my gut protocol that I've
[32:36] posted in your week seven
[32:39] course, these are the questions
[32:42] that go through that.
[32:44] I send that gut protocol to
[32:45] every patient that I see, every
[32:47] new patient that I see gets a
[32:49] copy of that gut protocol and
[32:51] they are supposed to go
[32:52] through it and evaluate
[32:54] themselves.
[32:54] Do you have more than one day
[32:55] a week you don't poop?
[32:57] If so, you are constipated.
[32:59] Do you have watery stools?
[33:00] You are constipated.
[33:01] Do you have hard balls for
[33:02] poop?
[33:03] You are constipated.
[33:04] Do you ever have explosive
[33:06] diarrhea?
[33:06] You are constipated.
[33:08] If you ever have tiny smears of
[33:09] blood on tissue when you're
[33:11] pooping, that's probably from a
[33:12] hemorrhoid.
[33:13] That's probably from
[33:14] constipation.
[33:15] Do you ever sit there or strain
[33:17] and push every time or
[33:19] sometimes when you're trying to
[33:21] poop?
[33:22] You should not have to strain.
[33:24] You shouldn't have to sit on
[33:25] the potty more than about eight
[33:26] or ten minutes.
[33:28] If you're sitting there that
[33:28] long, you have a gut
[33:29] dysfunction and you need to
[33:31] figure out why.
[33:33] You're only supposed to be
[33:34] emptying from the descending
[33:37] and the sigmoid colon.
[33:39] So if you're trying to empty
[33:40] your whole colon, that's not
[33:42] appropriate.
[33:43] Number one, it's not moving
[33:44] that fast.
[33:47] And so you should not have to
[33:48] strain or sit there for a long
[33:49] long time.
[33:50] Do you ever feel like you go
[33:52] to the bathroom and you sit
[33:53] there and you're pooping and
[33:54] you think, I think there's
[33:55] some more.
[33:56] I think I need to go some
[33:57] more, but you just it just
[33:59] won't happen.
[34:00] That is incomplete emptying and
[34:02] that is gut dysfunction.
[34:03] So if the patient has two
[34:05] yeses out of any of these
[34:08] questions, here's the rest of
[34:09] them, any two from both pages
[34:12] two at all, there's gut
[34:14] dysfunction, mostly
[34:16] constipation and they need to
[34:17] work on it.
[34:19] Do you ever feel like there's
[34:20] more poop inside that should
[34:21] be emptying similar to the
[34:23] previous question?
[34:24] Do you ever have lower
[34:25] abdominal pains and cramping?
[34:28] That should not happen.
[34:29] Do you ever feel bloated and
[34:31] overly full, even short of
[34:32] breath? Remember the transverse
[34:35] colon is sitting literally
[34:37] just under the diaphragm.
[34:39] What's above the diaphragm?
[34:40] Your lungs.
[34:41] So if there is poop sitting
[34:43] in that transverse colon,
[34:45] people will report shortness
[34:47] of breath and even
[34:48] palpitations.
[34:49] It will irritate their heart
[34:51] muscle sometimes.
[34:53] Do you suffer with reflux,
[34:54] indigestion or heartburn?
[34:56] Probably constipation.
[34:58] Have you reported
[35:00] diverticulosis or
[35:01] polyps? They are two different
[35:03] things, but have you seen
[35:07] diverticulosis or polyps
[35:11] on imaging or colonoscopy?
[35:12] That is evidence of chronic
[35:14] constipation.
[35:15] Poop is sitting too long and
[35:17] causing those pockets to swell
[35:20] and enlarge.
[35:21] And so if you answer yes to
[35:23] two or more of any of these
[35:25] questions, the problem is
[35:26] almost always chronic
[35:28] constipation.
[35:29] It can be subtle and you can
[35:31] still be pooping most days
[35:33] and have some sluggish gut
[35:35] motility.
[35:36] Maybe not full-blown
[35:37] constipation, but constipation
[35:39] gut function is on a spectrum
[35:41] that kind of is describing
[35:44] the Bristol stool form scale.
[35:46] It goes from very thick and
[35:48] hard ball all the way to
[35:50] watery, not solid at all.
[35:52] So that's kind of a spectrum.
[35:54] And so that's one reason
[35:55] we ask patients to work on
[35:57] the Bristol stool form scale
[35:59] and bring it back to clinic
[36:00] and let's take a look at it
[36:02] and see what is coming out
[36:04] because that will tell you
[36:05] what's happening along the
[36:06] track.
[36:08] Look at the entire digestive
[36:10] track.
[36:10] Are your patients actually
[36:12] chewing?

[36:15] each part of the digestive
[36:17] track.
[36:17] If they say they have a
[36:18] hiatal hernia, you don't

[36:21] health.
[36:22] But where is their symptom

[36:25] at. Those are the main

[36:28] gut health.
[36:31] So some absorption factors.

[36:36] because if their sodium
[36:38] number is 135, I can promise
[36:40] you their chloride is probably
[36:42] low as well and their stomach

[36:46] produced.

[36:49] Fats help cells heal and
[36:52] repair and if their gut is
[36:53] destroyed from SIBO or leaky
[36:56] gut or whatever, their mucosal
[37:00] lining along the GI track is
[37:02] probably also damaged and it
[37:04] needs some healing.
[37:05] Vitamin A and fats are two of
[37:06] the best ingredients for
[37:08] healing that tissue.
[37:10] Are they using PPIs and H2
[37:12] blockers?
[37:13] Many patients are using them
[37:14] over the counter for many
[37:15] weeks, many years, a long,
[37:17] long, long time and then
[37:19] they come to us and we
[37:20] wonder why their cell
[37:21] malnourished, their liver
[37:22] enzymes are all low, their

[37:24] proteins are all low, their

[37:30] H2 blockers actually block
[37:32] normal digestion.
[37:35] They're stopping acid
[37:36] production.
[37:37] If you stop acid production,
[37:40] how can you break down the
[37:43] food consumed for absorption in
[37:45] the small intestine?
[37:47] It can't.
[37:48] So patients that are on these
[37:50] have known and mainstream knows
[37:52] this, like boatloads of
[37:54] articles have come out.
[37:55] This is one of the reasons why
[37:57] PPIs and H2 blockers have
[37:59] black box warnings and are
[38:02] no longer recommended for
[38:03] long-term prescribing.
[38:05] They're meant for short-term
[38:06] use, like for H. pylori,
[38:09] for instance, something
[38:10] short-term, let's fix the
[38:11] problem, let's find the real
[38:13] reason you're having issues.
[38:15] Use these medicines while
[38:16] we're fixing the problem,
[38:17] but then you shouldn't use
[38:18] them anymore, but patients
[38:19] continue to use them because
[38:20] now they're easy to get.
[38:21] They're all over the
[38:22] counter.
[38:23] And so lots of times these
[38:26] PPIs and H2 blockers wind up
[38:28] causing way more problems
[38:30] because the patients don't
[38:31] realize they shouldn't be
[38:32] using the long-term.
[38:33] I think it's probably two of
[38:35] the classes of medicine that
[38:37] should have never gone over
[38:38] the counter, honestly.
[38:41] But that's a whole other
[38:41] showbox.
[38:42] Make sure they're chewing,
[38:43] make sure they're drinking
[38:45] plenty of fluids and make
[38:46] sure it's mostly water.
[38:49] Remember that caffeine will
[38:51] block some nutrition
[38:52] absorption, especially iron.
[38:54] And so we don't want them
[38:55] drinking tons of tea or
[38:57] caffeine, especially at the
[38:59] same time they're consuming

[39:02] poor nutrient absorption.
[39:04] Make sure their gut is moving.
[39:07] One of the big problems with
[39:08] GLP-1s today is it slows
[39:12] gut motility big time.
[39:13] It almost stops at like
[39:15] 98%.
[39:17] So that means the gut muscles
[39:18] are not contracting, that
[39:20] peristalsis is not happening.
[39:22] And so gut muscles are becoming
[39:25] lazier and lazier.
[39:27] And it's more sluggish and
[39:28] that allows for poop to sit
[39:30] inside those pockets and not
[39:32] flow through the system
[39:34] appropriately.
[39:35] When that happens, the bottom
[39:38] part of the digestive track
[39:40] is slow.
[39:41] And so where does that go?
[39:43] It begins to back up and
[39:45] back up and back up.
[39:46] And that is why we see
[39:47] reflux, heartburn, and
[39:48] indigestion with slow gut
[39:50] motility and constipation.
[39:53] We also see problems with
[39:54] microbiome health.
[39:56] Patients are unaware that
[39:58] that round of antibiotics
[39:59] they took for their
[40:00] cellulitis, their otitis,
[40:02] their sinusitis completely
[40:04] killed off all the healthy
[40:05] bacteria in their gut.
[40:07] And some of them are told
[40:09] you need to take a probiotic
[40:11] when you finish this or
[40:12] while you're taking this.
[40:14] So they go by something
[40:15] over-the-counter from
[40:17] the local grocery store that
[40:19] has one strain of bacteria
[40:21] and they think they're doing
[40:21] a great job to fix their gut.
[40:24] The gut is full of hundreds
[40:25] of strains of bacteria.
[40:27] So taking one strain is not
[40:29] likely to help repair that
[40:30] damage that the antibiotic did.
[40:33] I typically recommend a bare
[40:34] minimum of seven strains of
[40:36] bacteria in any probiotic
[40:38] that I recommend generally.
[40:41] There's a couple things that
[40:42] I do use that don't have

[40:46] for specific reasons and
[40:48] we'll talk about a few of
[40:48] those as we go through here.
[40:52] Then in addition to just
[40:54] normal malfunctions of digestion,
[40:58] we have all these medications
[41:00] and illnesses.
[41:02] So diabetes often alters
[41:04] gut motility as do all of
[41:06] these other diseases.
[41:09] And then the medications we
[41:10] use for all of these almost
[41:12] always cause gut dysfunction.
[41:13] Most of them include
[41:14] constipation as a side effect
[41:18] except for usually metformin.
[41:19] Metformin comes with diarrhea.
[41:21] But I have seen
[41:23] metformin cause constipation as well.
[41:26] So keep in mind that not only
[41:28] does the gut have dysfunction
[41:31] for reasons only related to
[41:34] cellular gut health,
[41:36] but diseases that patients
[41:39] have, chronic conditions that
[41:41] patients develop, and the
[41:43] medications for these will
[41:45] contribute to constipation.
[41:48] So if you have a patient on
[41:50] multiple drugs, look up
[41:52] the side effects and count
[41:54] how many of them list
[41:56] constipation as a side effect.
[41:58] I've had patients on five
[41:59] or six meds from other providers

[42:12] med list, I'm like, no
[42:12] wonder you're going once a week.
[42:14] You're taking five
[42:14] anticholinergic drugs.
[42:16] And so it literally dries the
[42:18] gut out and nobody told them
[42:20] they needed to work on
[42:21] improving their water intake,
[42:23] their electrolyte intake.
[42:24] So think about it.
[42:25] These are not just the only
[42:27] co-morbidities of digestive
[42:28] health. These are some of
[42:30] the most common that we see.
[42:34] SIBO is Small Intestinal
[42:36] Bacterial Overgrowth.
[42:38] It is a booger to heal and
[42:40] clean up.
[42:42] We do have treatment options.
[42:44] Ziphaxin, Rifaximin is
[42:47] typically $600 to $1,000 for
[42:49] a month of it and 30 days is
[42:51] the typical prescription
[42:53] recommendation.
[42:55] So that can really wreak havoc
[42:58] with people's financial state
[43:00] and their pocketbook.
[43:01] So that can be really
[43:03] challenging to get a hold of.
[43:05] Sometimes metronidazole or
[43:08] flagellomycin is sometimes
[43:10] prescribed for it instead.
[43:12] They are much less effective.
[43:15] But my favorite, if I got to
[43:16] use a treatment option and
[43:18] the patient can't afford

[43:20] metronidazole along with some
[43:24] of the functional agents.
[43:27] Garlic is a great antibiotic
[43:30] for SIBO.
[43:32] You can use neem.
[43:33] You can also get them on a
[43:35] low-carb or low-fodmap AIP
[43:38] eating plan.
[43:40] Those all reduce the risk
[43:43] that the grains specifically
[43:46] are going to ferment in the
[43:47] gut during that sluggish time
[43:51] that the food is sitting there
[43:52] inside the colon not going
[43:54] anywhere.
[43:55] So all of these things can help.
[43:58] If we have Candida, Candida
[44:00] is probably the most common gut
[44:02] problem we see.
[44:03] Why?
[44:04] Anybody want to guess why Candida
[44:06] is probably the most common?
[44:15] Who wants to take a guess?
[44:16] It's really simple.
[44:18] You're going to slap yourself
[44:19] if you don't get it right off
[44:20] the bat because you'll be like,
[44:21] Oh my God, I did that.
[44:24] Why would you think Candida
[44:26] might be the most common
[44:28] gut dysfunction?
[44:33] Yeah, it's the sugar.
[44:35] It's the high-carb intake.
[44:37] Carbs, think about yeast.
[44:39] Think about the overgrowth
[44:40] of yeast.
[44:41] Where does that occur?
[44:43] What do we feed yeast?
[44:44] If we're going to make bread,
[44:45] we're going to go in the kitchen.
[44:46] We're going to make some bread.
[44:47] What do we do?
[44:48] We mix yeast with sugar and we
[44:50] let it sit there for a little
[44:51] while until it foams and bubbles
[44:52] and then we mix up our bread.
[44:54] So what do you think is happening
[44:55] inside our gut?
[44:57] Most every Candida patient
[45:00] will tell you they have,
[45:01] they won't know they have yeast,
[45:03] but they will tell you and
[45:04] you'll be like,
[45:05] Oh my God, I know what's
[45:06] wrong with you.
[45:07] They will tell you that
[45:08] every time they eat,
[45:09] they feel bloated.
[45:11] They feel full.
[45:12] They get nauseous because it's
[45:13] just like everything's expanding.
[45:16] And so you'll be like,
[45:18] Oh, that's bloating is when yeast
[45:21] overgrow and they are producing
[45:22] extra gas and extra agents
[45:27] that make the gut stretch
[45:29] and feel uncomfortable.
[45:31] And so it's very common in your
[45:33] patients with insulin resistance
[45:34] diabetes and even in your high
[45:36] alcohol intake patients.
[45:39] So some of the treatment options
[45:41] for Candida, my favorite are
[45:44] caprylic acid Saccharomyces
[45:47] boulardii and this is the one
[45:48] time when I recommend a specific
[45:52] probiotic.
[45:52] It's a I forget.
[45:54] It's an ancient nutrition product.
[45:56] I think called SBO and it has
[46:00] a little bit of three different
[46:03] products.
[46:04] One is Saccharomyces boulardii
[46:06] and so I like it for patients
[46:09] that have some mild Candida.
[46:11] And so we'll use it for a few
[46:12] months and then we'll transition
[46:16] to another type of probiotic more
[46:18] long-term that will put healthy
[46:19] bacteria in but Saccharomyces
[46:21] boulardii is great for high-carb
[46:24] eating and Candida overgrowth
[46:27] because it really does help
[46:29] kill off those yeast.

[66:33] do anything else as time goes on.
[66:35] It's perfectly appropriate not
[66:37] to treat everything today.
[66:39] Do the best you can with what
[66:41] you have today.
[66:43] Have them work on something

[77:44] Fiber is a bulking agent.

[93:18] maybe the chronic root cause of their constipation and sluggish cut.
[93:22] And so, uh, getting them to understand that,
[93:26] not that they need to stop their medicine.

[93:32] more salt, more butter, or using more magnesium.

[93:46] cause some mildly loose stool.

[98:07] Like.

[99:30] citrate.
[99:32] Two or three times a day.
[99:37] But low doses, like 150, 100, 150 milligrams of mag citrate.

[99:45] The liquid form is more of a laxative.
[99:49] The low doses of mag citrate will help the body manage the formation
[99:57] of kidney stones much better.
